meta.paper: FAPO: Fully Autonomous Prompt Optimization of Multi-Step LLM Pipelines

research_equation.problem: Multi-step LLM pipelines fail through error propagation across retrieval, reasoning, formatting, and control flow steps. Prompt-only optimization cannot diagnose or fix structural bottlenecks in the chain.
research_equation.existing_solution: GEPA, DSPy, and other prompt-programming systems optimize prompts within fixed pipeline structures. They lack step-level failure attribution and cannot modify chain topology.
research_equation.gap: No existing system combines step-level failure attribution, scope-constrained multi-level optimization (prompt → parameter → structure), and agentic workspace-level iteration in a reproducible, guardrailed framework.
research_equation.proposed_solution: FAPO uses Claude Code to drive a closed-loop optimization cycle: evaluate pipeline → attribute failures via intermediate step inspection → propose scoped changes → review → validate variants. It starts with prompt edits and escalates to structural changes only when evidence shows prompt optimization is insufficient.
research_equation.key_claim: Pipeline-aware, evidence-grounded optimization with prompt-first escalation beats prompt-only search (15/18 benchmark wins, +14.1 pp mean gain) and can achieve even larger gains (+33.8 pp) when structural changes are needed.

first_pass.what: FAPO is a Claude Code-based framework for autonomous optimization of multi-step LLM pipelines. It treats the pipeline as an inspectable workflow, records intermediate step outputs, attributes failures to specific steps, and iterates through prompt → parameter → structure optimization levels under tenant-defined guardrails.
contributions: Claude Code-based pipeline optimization with prompt-first escalation to structural changes
Reproducible workspace procedure with intermediate step recording and variant history
Demonstrated performance gains across QA, fact verification, instruction following, math, and security classification
strengths: Clear, well-motivated problem (prompt-only optimization misses structural bottlenecks)
Strong empirical results (15/18 wins, detailed statistical reporting)
Practical tenant isolation model for enterprise deployment
Thoughtful guardrails against overfitting (split access, scope contracts, iteration memory, variant immutability)
Security domain results add credibility beyond benchmarks
weaknesses: Comparison with GEPA is not fully controlled (FAPO has broader search space by design)
AIME results inconclusive — suggests scope for improvement in math reasoning optimization
Single orchestrator model (Claude Code) — unclear how FAPO generalizes to other coding agents
Higher variance when structural changes are allowed due to path dependence

second_pass.method_family: Agent-driven closed-loop optimization over discrete LLM pipeline configurations, with attribution-guided search
key_techniques: Claude Code as orchestrator with three specialized agents (optimization, step-attribution, variant-reviewer)
LangGraph-based pipeline representation for step-level inspection
Tenant workspace model with playbook-defined scope contracts
Prompt-first escalation policy for controlled optimization levels
Heuristic + LLM-based failure attribution for root cause localization
Independent reviewer subagent for safety and scope compliance
second_pass.evaluation_protocol: Three trials per model-benchmark cell, best validation-selected variant reported with mean ± trial std dev. GEPA comparison starts from identical baseline pipelines. FAPO budget: 50 variants or 10 rounds per trial.

unresolved_questions: How does FAPO scale with more complex pipelines (10+ nodes)?
Can FAPO optimize pipelines with non-deterministic or agentic steps (tool use, sub-agents)?
How sensitive are results to the orchestrator model (Claude Code vs alternatives)?
What is the cost (in API calls, tokens, wall time) of FAPO relative to GEPA?
Can the escalation rules be learned rather than heuristically determined?
How does FAPO handle pipelines with human-in-the-loop steps?
threats_to_validity: Broader search space for FAPO than GEPA makes comparison non-apples-to-apples
Single orchestrator model limits generalizability claims
AIME negative results unexplained — possible overfitting to small dev set
GEPA reproduction uses different reflector model than original paper
Token budget asymmetry between GPT-4.1-mini and GPT-5.4-mini complicates cross-model analysis
No ablation study isolating prompt-only vs structural-only FAPO contributions

challenge_to_module_map.how_addressed: Records every node's output in step_outputs dict; attribution analyzes step names, output content, empty steps, and cascading failures

challenge_to_module_map.challenge: Prompt-only optimization cannot fix structural bottlenecks
challenge_to_module_map.fapo_module: Prompt-first escalation policy with scope contracts
challenge_to_module_map.how_addressed: Escalates from prompt edits → chain parameters → chain structure only when attribution indicates structural bottleneck and scope permits

challenge_to_module_map.fapo_module: Four guardrails (split access, scope constraints, iteration memory, variant immutability)
challenge_to_module_map.how_addressed: Optimizer sees only training cases; validation/test expose aggregates only; every variant is immutable and logged
Each tenant has isolated directories for chains, prompts, configs, datasets; shared runtime enforces path- and module-level isolation

challenge_to_module_map.challenge: Verification that changes respect task constraints
challenge_to_module_map.how_addressed: Independently checks scope compliance, placeholder integrity, data leakage, scorer compatibility, state protocol, and import safety
